did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a two-stream deep neural network architecture for multiclass skin cancer classification. The method involves a fusion-based contrast enhancement technique feeding into a pretrained DenseNet201 architecture, feature optimization using a skewness-controlled moth-flame optimization algorithm, and deep feature extraction from a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 network. An extreme learning machine classifier is used to classify lesion images from dermoscopic datasets (HAM10000, ISBI2018, ISIC2019).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. It presents original experimental work, including a proposed two-stream deep neural network architecture, feature optimization algorithms, and classification results on three specific datasets (HAM10000, ISBI2018, ISIC2019) with reported accuracy metrics (96.5%, 98%, 89%). The structure includes standard research sections such as Introduction, Data Sets, Proposed Method, Experimental Results, and Conclusion, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A two‐stream deep neural network‐based intelligent system for complex skin cancer types classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a two-stream deep neural network framework for multiclass skin cancer classification using dermoscopic images. The first stream employs a fusion-based contrast enhancement technique feeding into a pretrained DenseNet201 architecture, with features optimized via a skewness-controlled moth-flame optimization algorithm. The second stream extracts deep features from a fine-tuned MobileNetV2 network, which are down-sampled and fused with the first stream's features using a parallel multimax coefficient correlation method. An extreme learning machine classifier is utilized to categorize lesions from three imbalanced datasets: HAM10000, ISBI2018, and ISIC2019. The system achieved classification accuracies of 96.5%, 98%, and 89% on these respective datasets without data augmentation. Comparative analysis demonstrates that the proposed algorithm offers improved performance over existing techniques.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass skin cancer types (actinic keratoses, basal cell carcinoma, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, melanoma, nevi, vascular lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images into seven diagnostic categories using a two-stream deep neural network and an extreme learning machine (MCELM) classifier. The task is explicitly defined as 'multiclass skin cancer types classification' and 'multiclass skin lesion classification' on datasets HAM10000, ISBI2018, and ISIC2019.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, publicly available benchmark dataset.

use.training: Used for training the proposed two-stream deep neural network framework with a 50:50 training/testing ratio.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework, achieving 96.5% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISBI2018
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset for classification tasks, publicly available benchmark dataset.

use.training: Used for training the proposed two-stream deep neural network framework with a 50:50 training/testing ratio.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework, achieving 98% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: ISIC-2019
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset, publicly available benchmark dataset.

use.training: Used for training the proposed two-stream deep neural network framework with a 50:50 training/testing ratio.
use.testing: Used for testing the proposed framework, achieving 89% accuracy.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the performance of the classifiers and obtain the reported accuracy, sensitivity, precision, and F1-Score metrics.
To validate classification results and identify misclassification rates for specific skin classes (e.g., DF and VASC).
To compare the proposed algorithm's performance against existing techniques and other classifiers (MCSVM, GN Bayes, W-KNN, Softmax, EBT).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1-Score) and computational time obtained via 10-fold cross-validation.

HAM10000.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-Score, Computation Time
HAM10000.notes: The proposed framework achieved the best accuracy and sensitivity. The lowest sensitivity was obtained by GN Bayes. Confusion matrix analysis indicated low accuracy for akiec and vasc classes. The relation coefficient of 0.4 yielded the best accuracy (96.5%). Comparison with other feature selection techniques (Auto-Encoder, GA, PSO, ACO) showed MMFO provided superior results.

ISBI2018.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-Score, Computation Time
ISBI2018.notes: The proposed framework achieved the highest accuracy, precision, and F1-Score. The minimum sensitivity was obtained by EBT. The minimum computation time was achieved by Softmax. Confusion matrix analysis showed high misclassification for DF and VASC classes due to fewer images. The relation coefficient of 0.4 yielded the best accuracy (98.0%).

ISIC2019.metric: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Precision, F1-Score, Computation Time
ISIC2019.notes: The proposed framework achieved the best accuracy. Softmax achieved the second-best accuracy (88.3%) and the minimum computation time (102.176 s). The relation coefficient of 0.4 yielded the best accuracy (89.0%). Iteration-based analysis (100 iterations) showed consistent accuracy for HAM10000 and ISBI2018 compared to ISIC2019.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stream deep neural network framework with Multiclass Extreme Learning Machine (MCELM) classifier
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet201 and MobileNetV2 pretrained on ImageNet dataset

stream_2.architecture_structure: Inverted residual connections, bottleneck layers with 1x1, 3x3 depthwise, and 1x1 pointwise convolutions

classification_or_output_head.notes: Original Softmax and fully connected layers of pretrained models were removed and replaced with new layers for 8 skin cancer categories

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Two-Stream Deep Neural Network Framework
preprocessing_techniques: Fusion-based contrast enhancement technique (local-global information fusion)
Bottom-hat-controlled local mean (BHcLM) for local contrast enhancement
Bottom-hat transform with dynamic structural element
Modified top-hat transformation for global contrast enhancement
Explicit exclusion of segmentation steps (preprocessing and segmentation not performed for multiclass classification in this study)
conclusions: The proposed two-stream deep neural network framework achieved classification accuracies of 96.5%, 98%, and 89% on the HAM10000, ISBI2018, and ISIC2019 datasets, respectively, without using data augmentation.
The proposed algorithm demonstrated improved performance compared to existing techniques and other classifiers (MCSVM, GN Bayes, W-KNN, Softmax, EBT) across all tested datasets.

The fusion of features from both streams using the parallel multimax coefficient correlation method significantly improved accuracy compared to using individual streams (Stream 1 selected feature accuracy: 92.3% on HAM10000, 93.9% on ISBI2018; Stream 2 selected feature accuracy: 93.8% on HAM10000, 94.6% on ISBI2018).
The skewness-controlled moth-flame optimization (MMFO) algorithm effectively selected discriminant features, outperforming other feature selection techniques such as Auto-Encoder, GA, PSO, and ACO.
The Multiclass Extreme Learning Machine (MCELM) classifier provided the best sensitivity and accuracy results among the tested classifiers.

Classes with fewer images, specifically dermatofibroma (DF) and vascular lesion (VASC), exhibited higher misclassification rates due to class imbalance, although this had a limited impact on overall classification performance.

The proposed method achieved competitive or superior computational efficiency, with the lowest computation time on HAM10000 (10.761 s) compared to other classifiers, though it was not the fastest on ISBI2018 (Softmax was fastest at 19.251 s) or ISIC2019 (Softmax was fastest at 102.176 s).
important_remarks: The study explicitly excludes data augmentation, which is a significant limitation given the highly imbalanced nature of the datasets (e.g., HAM10000 has 6705 nevi vs. 115 dermatofibroma), potentially inflating performance on majority classes.

The paper does not perform lesion segmentation, relying instead on full-image classification; the authors acknowledge in the conclusion that adding segmentation could refine results, indicating a methodological gap.

Performance varies significantly across datasets (96.5% on HAM10000, 98% on ISBI2018, but drops to 89% on ISIC2019), suggesting potential dataset bias or sensitivity to the larger, more complex ISIC2019 distribution.

Classes with fewer images, specifically dermatofibroma (DF) and vascular lesion (VASC), exhibited higher misclassification rates due to class imbalance, although the authors claim this had a limited impact on overall performance.

The computational burden increases with the fusion process; while the proposed method is efficient on smaller datasets (10.761s on HAM10000), it is slower than Softmax on larger datasets (122.621s on ISIC2019 vs. Softmax's 102.176s).

No external validation was performed; all models were trained and tested on the same three public datasets (HAM10000, ISBI2018, ISIC2019) using 10-fold cross-validation, limiting generalizability assessment.
The paper does not report statistical significance tests (p-values) for the comparisons with existing techniques, relying solely on descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, precision, F1-Score).

The proposed feature selection algorithm (Skewness-controlled Moth-Flame Optimization) and fusion method (Parallel Multimax Coefficient Correlation) are novel contributions, but their specific hyperparameters (e.g., relation coefficient of 0.4) were tuned empirically without extensive ablation studies on parameter sensitivity.
